question: Create two classes, `DataHolder` and `Processor`. `DataHolder` has a private integer member `value`. Implement a function `processData(DataHolder& dh)` which needs to access and modify `DataHolder`'s private `value`. Declare `processData` as a `friend` function of the `DataHolder` class to enable this access.

test_cases.expected:
--- Test Case 1: Uninitialized DataHolder ---
Initial state (uninitialized value): DataHolder value: <system-dependent-garbage>
processData: Attempted to increment value.
After processData (uninitialized): DataHolder value: <system-dependent-garbage-plus-1>

--- Test Case 2: Potential Overflow ---
Initial state (INT_MAX): DataHolder value: 2147483647
processData: Attempted to increment value.
After processData (INT_MAX + 1): DataHolder value: -2147483648

driver:
// The main function in the provided C++ code serves as the driver.
// It demonstrates operating on an uninitialized DataHolder and triggering integer overflow.
errors: The `DataHolder` class does not provide a constructor to initialize its `value` member, leading to undefined behavior if `processData` is called on a default-constructed object without prior explicit initialization of `value`.
The `processData` function increments `DataHolder::value` without checking for potential integer overflow. When `value` is `INT_MAX`, incrementing it results in undefined behavior.
